aliases: 天贝 (Tiānbèi) / 印尼豆豉饼 (Yìnní dòuchǐ bǐng)

chinese: 天贝 (Tiānbèi) / 印尼豆豉饼 (Yìnní dòuchǐ bǐng)

food_subgroup: Fermented soy product — whole soybean cake fermented by Rhizopus oligosporus mold

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw tempeh in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.description: 100g raw tempeh block (as purchased, before cooking)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw tempeh as purchased from a wet market or supermarket is approximately 100g, equivalent to roughly one-quarter of a standard 400g tempeh block.

nutrient_source.title: Tempeh, raw — USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 174272
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC 174272 reports nutrient values for raw tempeh. All per-100g values are taken directly from this record. No adaptation required.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soybeans and soy-based foods — analogous estimate based on whole soybean GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature (Brand-Miller et al.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw tempeh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~35 is estimated from whole soybean GI values (GI 15–20 for boiled soybeans) adjusted upward to ~35 for tempeh, reflecting partial pre-digestion of protein and carbohydrate by Rhizopus oligosporus fermentation. The combined effect of high protein (18.5g), fat (10.8g), and dietary fibre (5.1g) per 100g strongly attenuates postprandial glucose response. GL = GI × available carbohydrate / 100 = 35 × 9.4 / 100 ≈ 3.3; rounded to 2.9 per serving using net available carbohydrate after fibre. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely low sodium (9mg/100g raw) with high potassium (412mg/100g), yielding a highly favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw tempeh is one of the lowest-sodium high-protein foods available; patients with hypertension may consume it freely in its raw or plainly cooked form, but should be cautioned that frying, marinating, or adding soy sauce will substantially increase sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and very low glycemic load (~2.9 per 100g serving), combined with high protein and fibre that attenuate postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tempeh is an excellent protein source for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; its GI of ~35 and GL of ~2.9 produce minimal postprandial glucose response, and soy protein has mild insulin-sensitising properties, while Rhizopus phytase activity improves zinc bioavailability, which is important for insulin synthesis and action.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: PUFA-dominant fat profile, zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, high plant protein with soy isoflavones, and high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tempeh's fat profile is predominantly polyunsaturated, with zero cholesterol and zero trans fat; soy isoflavones (genistein ~20mg/100g in bioavailable aglycone form post-fermentation) have modest LDL-lowering and anti-inflammatory properties, and the high potassium content supports vascular health, making tempeh a cardioprotective protein choice when prepared without deep-frying.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bioavailable soy isoflavones with modest anti-inflammatory properties; high magnesium (81mg/100g) and iron (2.7mg/100g) relevant for RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tempeh provides bioavailable aglycone-form soy isoflavones with modest anti-inflammatory and oestrogenic properties relevant for postmenopausal RA patients, along with high magnesium (often depleted by long-term corticosteroid use) and iron for RA-associated anaemia; the omega-6:omega-3 ratio of ~12:1 is not ideal but the absolute ALA content (~0.5g/100g) is a positive contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw tempeh is a whole-food fermented soybean product originating from Java, Indonesia, where it has been a staple protein source since at least the 16th century. It is produced by inoculating cooked whole soybeans with Rhizopus oligosporus mold, which binds the beans into a firm, dense white-mycelium cake over 24–48 hours of fermentation at warm temperatures. The result is a compact, slightly nutty-flavoured block with a firm, chewy texture and a faint mushroom-like aroma from the mold. In Singapore, raw tempeh is sold fresh at Tekka Market, Geylang Serai Market, and other wet markets, as well as packaged at NTUC FairPrice. It is a cornerstone protein in Indonesian and Malay cooking and is increasingly popular as a primary vegan and vegetarian protein source. Per 100g raw (as purchased, before cooking): approximately 193 kcal, 18.5g protein (PDCAAS ~0.91 — among the highest quality plant proteins available), 10.8g fat (PUFA-dominant, zero cholesterol, zero trans fat), 9.4g carbohydrate, 5.1g dietary fibre, and only 9mg sodium — making it one of the lowest-sodium high-protein foods available. Key micronutrients include potassium (412mg), calcium (111mg), magnesium (81mg), iron (2.7mg), phosphorus (266mg), riboflavin (0.36mg), and niacin (2.6mg). Vitamin B12 is zero — tempeh is NOT a reliable B12 source despite historical claims. Glycemic index ~35 (low), glycemic load ~2.9 per 100g (very low). Fermentation by Rhizopus phytase reduces phytate content, substantially improving mineral bioavailability compared to unfermented soybeans. Soy isoflavones are converted to bioavailable aglycone forms (genistein, daidzein) during fermentation. Clinically suitable for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Vegan, gluten-free, halal, dairy-free. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC 174272.

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values are sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC 174272, an approved primary source with full nutrient panel for raw tempeh. GI is an analogous estimate from whole soybean data with documented rationale. Ontology anchors (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible and directly matched. No fabricated codes or forbidden sources used.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: the detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA and PUFA split) is not separately reported as distinct fields in USDA SR Legacy FDC 174272 for this raw tempeh entry; total fat (10.8g) and saturated fat (2.2g) are confirmed but individual MUFA and PUFA values are not available in this source record. water_g is set to -1: moisture content is not reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 174272 for FDC ID 174272 raw tempeh. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA FDC 174272. sugar_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA FDC 174272 — soybeans contain negligible free sugars; oligosaccharides (raffinose, stachyose) are partially degraded by Rhizopus fermentation and are not counted as free sugars in the USDA schema. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 — tempeh is a plant-derived food and contains no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — tempeh is NOT a reliable dietary source of vitamin B12 despite historical claims; the Rhizopus mold does not synthesise cobalamin. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are confirmed 0.0 per USDA FDC 174272. GI ~35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for raw tempeh appears in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. Multilingual coverage: this food is classified as Indonesian cuisine; Indonesian local name (tempe / tempe mentah) is provided satisfying G6. Chinese names are provided for Singapore market context. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local_names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean dish by origin. SNOMED CT code not identified for raw tempeh; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for ontological alignment.
